source_paper_title: Simple and Explicit Bounds for Multiserver Queues with 1/1−ρ Scaling
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Li_2025, title={Simple and Explicit Bounds for Multiserver Queues with 1/1−ρ Scaling}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0131}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0131}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Li, Yuan and Goldberg, David A.}, year={2025}, month=may, pages={813–837} }
context:
Page 22, Section 3 ("Towards bounds with no large prefactors"), immediately after Theorem 9 (Conjecture 1).

Conjecture 1. In any M/G/n queue in which \(E[S^2] < \infty\) and all relevant steady-state distributions exist, it holds that
\[E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)]\times E[\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)] \le E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\times \mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)],\]
and hence \(E[L(\infty)] \le \tfrac12 E[(S\mu_S)^2]\,\frac{\rho}{1-\rho}.\]
We leave a further study as an interesting open question, and note that tools from the theory of associated r.v.s (which have been used to prove related results in the literature) may be relevant here (see e.g. Baccelli et al. [9]).
title: Prove covariance inequality between number in service and work in M GI n queues
background_latex:
Consider a first-come-first-served (FCFS) many-server queue with Poisson arrivals and general i.i.d. service times.

1) Model. An M/GI/n queue has:
1) arrivals given by a Poisson process of rate \(\lambda>0\);
2) \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) identical servers;
3) i.i.d. service times \(S\) with \(0<E[S]<\infty\) and \(E[S^2]<\infty\);
4) FCFS discipline, no abandonments.

Let \(\mu_S = 1/E[S]\) denote the service rate of a single server, and define the traffic intensity
\[\rho := \frac{\lambda}{n\mu_S} = \frac{\lambda E[S]}{n},\qquad 0<\rho<1.\]
Assume the system is stable and admits a steady state.

2) Steady-state quantities. Let \(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\) be the steady-state number of busy servers (number in service). Let \(\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\) be the steady-state total remaining workload in service, i.e. the sum of the residual service times of all jobs currently in service (with idle servers contributing \(0\)).

Equivalently, if \(R_1(\infty),\dots,R_n(\infty)\) are the (random) residual service times in steady state on each server (with \(R_j(\infty)=0\) when server \(j\) is idle), then
\[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)=\sum_{j=1}^n \mathbf{1}\{R_j(\infty)>0\},\qquad \mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)=\sum_{j=1}^n R_j(\infty).\]

3) Covariance interpretation. The inequality
\[E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)]\,E[\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)] \le E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\,\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)]\]
is equivalent to \(\mathrm{Cov}(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty),\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty))\ge 0\), i.e. nonnegative correlation between the number of busy servers and the total residual workload in service.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish conditions under which, for an FCFS \(\mathrm{M}/\mathrm{GI}/n\) queue with \(E[S^2]<\infty\) in steady state,
\[E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)]\,E[\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)] \le E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\,\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)].\]
Equivalently, prove that
\[\mathrm{Cov}(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty),\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty))\ge 0\]
for this class of queues (assuming the relevant steady-state distributions exist).
latex:
Consider a first-come-first-served (FCFS) many-server queue with Poisson arrivals and general i.i.d. service times.

1) Model. An M/GI/n queue has:
1) arrivals given by a Poisson process of rate \(\lambda>0\);
2) \(n\in\mathbb{N}\) identical servers;
3) i.i.d. service times \(S\) with \(0<E[S]<\infty\) and \(E[S^2]<\infty\);
4) FCFS discipline, no abandonments.

Let \(\mu_S = 1/E[S]\) denote the service rate of a single server, and define the traffic intensity

Let \(\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\) be the steady-state total remaining workload in service, i.e. the sum of the residual service times of all jobs currently in service (with idle servers contributing \(0\)).

Establish conditions under which, for an FCFS \(\mathrm{M}/\mathrm{GI}/n\) queue with \(E[S^2]<\infty\) in steady state,
\[E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)]\,E[\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)] \le E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\,\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)].\]

\[\mathrm{Cov}(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty),\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty))\ge 0\]
for this class of queues (assuming the relevant steady-state distributions exist).
location_in_paper: Page 22, Section 3 ("Towards bounds with no large prefactors"), immediately after Theorem 9 (Conjecture 1).
open_problem_quote:
Conjecture 1. In any M/G/n queue in which \(E[S^2] < \infty\) and all relevant steady-state distributions exist, it holds that
\[E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)]\times E[\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)] \le E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)\times \mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty)],\]
and hence \(E[L(\infty)] \le \tfrac12 E[(S\mu_S)^2]\,\frac{\rho}{1-\rho}.\]
We leave a further study as an interesting open question, and note that tools from the theory of associated r.v.s (which have been used to prove related results in the literature) may be relevant here (see e.g. Baccelli et al. [9]).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can simulate M/GI/n queues for specific service-time distributions and estimate the covariance between number-in-service and work-in-service; however, turning a suspected violation into a rigorous counterexample is difficult because it concerns an exact steady-state inequality for a continuous-state Markov process.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could search over parametric service-time distributions (e.g., mixtures, hyperexponential, deterministic-plus-atom) and server counts to maximize negative empirical covariance as a heuristic fitness signal. This could plausibly find candidate counterexamples or suggest strengthened conditions, but it would not by itself prove the inequality or certify a violation.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Li--Goldberg (arXiv:1706.04628v3), the covariance inequality \(\mathrm{Cov}(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty),\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty))\ge 0\) is posed as Conjecture 1 because it would remove the negative correction term in their drift identity (Theorem 9) and yield the clean bound \(\mathbb E[L(\infty)]\le \tfrac12\mathbb E[(S\mu_S)^2]\,\rho/(1-\rho)\) for \(M/GI/n\). The conjecture concerns the steady-state joint distribution of the vector of residual service times \((R_1(\infty),\dots,R_n(\infty))\), specifically the mixed moment \(\mathbb E[\sum_j \mathbf 1\{R_j>0\}\,\sum_j R_j]\), and asks for a positive-association type statement under FCFS with Poisson arrivals and \(\mathbb E[S^2]<\infty\).

The limited forward-citation evidence provided does not contain a proof or counterexample. The most relevant forward-citing work, “A new -scaling bound for multiserver queues via a leave-one-out technique,” develops a BAR-based decomposition for steady-state means in \(GI/GI/n\) and introduces leave-one-out couplings to control boundary terms that are explicitly interpreted as covariance-like corrections between indicators of boundary events (e.g. emptiness) and residual service quantities. While this does not establish a sign for \(\mathrm{Cov}(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}},\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}})\), it suggests a plausible strategy: represent \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}\,\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}]\) via a BAR/Poisson equation and attempt to show that the relevant boundary term is nonnegative (or asymptotically negligible) using conditional independence/monotonicity arguments.

“Novel Lower Bounds on M/G/k Scheduling” is methodologically related through its BAR/drift analysis of workload-type processes and explicit moment calculations in surrogate models, but it does not engage with FCFS many-server residual-service vectors or positive correlation properties. Overall, based on the provided citation set, the conjectured covariance inequality appears to remain open; progress seems to be primarily methodological (BAR decompositions and couplings) rather than a direct resolution. Promising directions include exploiting association/positive dependence results for FCFS queues (e.g. via Palm calculus and monotone couplings) to show that conditioning on more busy servers stochastically increases the residual workload in service, or proving the inequality under additional structural assumptions on \(S\) (e.g. decreasing hazard rate, log-convex tails) and then extending by approximation.

sources.title: A new -scaling bound for multiserver queues via a leave-one-out technique
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves new universal steady-state mean queue-length bounds of order \(O(1/(1-\rho))\) for the FCFS \(GI/GI/n\) queue (via a modified system), e.g. for \(M/GI/n\): \(E_\pi[Q]\le R_s^{\max}/(1-\rho)\) under light-tail/non-lattice/positive Harris recurrence assumptions, using BAR and a leave-one-out coupling to control boundary “covariance” terms.
sources.connection: The open problem asks to prove \(\mathrm{Cov}(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty),\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty))\ge 0\) in steady state for FCFS \(M/GI/n\). This paper does not study \(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}\) or \(\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}\) directly, nor does it establish a nonnegative covariance between them. It does, however, derive BAR-based decompositions for \(E_\pi[Q]\) involving boundary terms \(\Gamma_{s,j}=E_s[\mathbf 1\{Q=0\}\,\mu R_{s,j}]-(1-\rho)E_\pi[\mu R_{s,j}]\), which it heuristically interprets as covariance-like quantities between \(\mathbf 1\{Q=0\}\) and residual service times, and then bounds them via a leave-one-out asymptotic-independence argument. These techniques may be adaptable to analyze correlations between service occupancy and in-service workload, but no such covariance inequality is proved here.

sources.title: Novel Lower Bounds on M/G/k Scheduling
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces the Increasing Speed Queue (ISQ-k), a single-server variable-speed model, and proves workload/relevant-work lower bounds for M/G/k queues under arbitrary (possibly preemptive) scheduling policies. Key statements include: - Proposition 6.1 / Theorem 4.3: for any arrival/job-size sample path, the total work in any k-server system is lower bounded by the total work in ISQ-k; hence in steady state, for Poisson arrivals, \(\mathbb E[W^{M/G/k}]\ge \mathbb E[W^{ISQ-k}]\). - Theorems 6.26.3: the mean SRPT-relevant work \(\mathbb E[W_x]\) in M/G/k under any policy is lower bounded by that in auxiliary Sep-ISQ-k and (for some recycling stream) Rec-ISQ-k. - Propositions 8.3 and 10.3: explicit formulas for \(\mathbb E[W^{ISQ-2}]\) and \(\mathbb E[W^{ISQ-k}]\) via a BAR/drift method with differential-equation-derived test functions (DiffeDrift).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a nonnegative covariance inequality between the steady-state number in service and the total residual workload in service in an FCFS M/GI/n queue: \(\mathrm{Cov}(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty),\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}(\infty))\ge 0\). This paper does not study FCFS M/GI/n steady-state joint distributions of (busy servers, residual service workload), nor does it address covariance/association properties. Its main objects are (i) mean response time lower bounds under arbitrary scheduling in M/G/k, (ii) SRPT-relevant work integrals (WINE), and (iii) total work in system (sum of remaining job sizes) in a variable-speed single-server surrogate. While it develops BAR/drift techniques for variable-rate workload processes that could be conceptually useful in analyzing moments of workload-like quantities, it provides no result implying \(\mathbb E[\mathrm{Num}]\,\mathbb E[\mathrm{Work}]\le \mathbb E[\mathrm{Num}\cdot\mathrm{Work}]\) for FCFS M/GI/n, and it does not even define \(\mathrm{Num}_{\mathrm{service}}\) and \(\mathrm{Work}_{\mathrm{service}}\) in the many-server FCFS sense.
